title: The Twenty Fifth Sunday after Pentecost
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
## Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
## Sermon Date: November 11, 2018

---

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This is the Twenty-Fifth Sunday after Pentecost in the Church Year, falling in the season of Ordinary Time and approaching the final Sundays before Advent (which begins November 25, 2018). The Revised Common Lectionary readings for this Sunday would include 1 Thessalonians 5:1-11 and Matthew 25:14-30 (the Parable of the Talents), along with Zephaniah 1:7, 12-18 as the Old Testament reading. The liturgical color would be green, and this is one of the last Sundays of the liturgical year before the cycle turns toward Advent and Christ the King Sunday on November 25.

---

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in the period immediately following the 79th General Convention held in Austin, Texas in July 2018, where significant decisions were made regarding church polity and social issues. In the Diocese of Colorado, the church would have been operating under Bishop Skipper Schwalm's leadership (he served from 2013-2022). There were no major diocesan bishop elections or unusual controversies immediately surrounding this November 2018 date, though the larger church continued discussions about marriage equality and LGBTQ+ inclusion that had been ongoing since the 2012 General Convention.

---

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

November 11, 2018 was Veterans Day, likely creating a community observance in Denver with particular solemnity. The week prior saw Denver in early winter weather patterns with temperatures in the 40s-50s Fahrenheit. Locally, Colorado was navigating the first full year after the legalization of recreational marijuana (legal since January 2014) with ongoing regulatory questions. The Capitol Hill neighborhood, where Saint John's Cathedral is located, was experiencing continued gentrification and development pressure typical of downtown Denver during this mid-to-late 2010s period of rapid growth.

---

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS

The midterm elections had just concluded three days prior on November 6, 2018, with Democrats gaining 40 House seats and Republicans retaining the Senate; the national mood reflected intense political division and turnout that exceeded 110 million voters. The Trump administration was in the midst of significant personnel changes, with James Mattis still serving as Secretary of Defense but significant tension with the President over foreign policy decisions. The #MeToo movement continued as a major cultural and political force, with investigations and sexual harassment allegations continuing to dominate news cycles across entertainment, politics, and business sectors.

---

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The migrant caravan traveling from Central America toward the U.S.-Mexico border was a major international news story during this period, with approximately 7,000-8,000 migrants from Honduras, Guatemala, and El Salvador traveling through Mexico toward the U.S., triggering significant political debate and military deployments. The Camp Fire was igniting in Northern California (beginning November 8, 2018), becoming the deadliest and most destructive wildfire in California history, with news coverage intensifying during this exact week. The Yemen humanitarian crisis continued with limited international coverage, though the Saudi-led coalition's bombing campaign and resulting famine affected millions of civilians.

---

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

The national mood in mid-November 2018 reflected deep political polarization in the wake of the midterm elections, with Americans processing the Democratic House gains and wondering about the 2020 presidential race that was beginning to take shape. The holiday season was beginning with Thanksgiving just two weeks away, creating a cultural moment of reflection on gratitude and national divisions. In Denver specifically, the Broncos were mid-season (finishing 6-10 in 2018), and the city's general cultural moment was one of confident growth and development, though awareness of climate and fire danger was increasing due to the California wildfires receiving national attention.
